en.extract:
The green-tailed jacamar (Galbula galbula) is a species of bird in the family Galbulidae. It is native to Brazil, Colombia, French Guiana, Guyana, Suriname, and Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The green-tailed jacamar is monotypic. It and the rufous-tailed (Galbula ruficauda), white-chinned (G. tombacea), bluish-fronted (G. cyanescens), and coppery-chested jacamars (G. pastazae) are considered to form a superspecies.


Description
The green-tailed jacamar is 18 to 22 cm (7.1 to 8.7 in) long and weighs 18 to 29 g (0.63 to 1.02 oz). The male's head, upperparts, and a band across the chest are metallic coppery green, somewhat bluer on the face. The tail is bluish green. The throat is white and the belly and vent area reddish chestnut. The female is similar but the throat is buff and the underparts are duller and paler.


Distribution and habitat
The green-tailed jacamar is found from eastern Colombia's Vichada and Meta Departments east through southern and eastern Venezuela south of the Orinoco River to the Guianas and south in Brazil to the lower Madeira and Tapajós Rivers. It inhabits the borders, but not the interior, of several humid forest types including terra firme, várzea, gallery, and mangrove. It prefers open woodland, savanna, and shrublands, especially along watercourses. In elevation it ranges from sea level to 700 m (2,300 ft).


Behavior


Feeding
The green-tailed jacamar's diet is a large variety of insects though Hymenoptera predominate. It perches on exposed branches, often in pairs, and sallies from there to catch its flying prey.


Breeding
The green-tailed jacamar excavates burrows in earth banks or arboreal termite nests. It has been documented breeding February to March in Venezuela, in May, June, and August in Suriname, and in April and September in Brazil.


Vocalization
The green-tailed jacamar's song is an "accelerating 'peeo peeo peea pee-pee-pee-pee-pe-pe-pe-e-e-e-e'e'e'e' that ends in a trill [1]. Its call is a repeated "peep" or "peer", sometimes in a series [2].


Status
The IUCN has assessed the green-tailed jacamar as being of Least Concern. It appears to be common in most of its range and occurs in several protected areas.


References


External links
Green-tailed jacamar videos on the Internet Bird Collection
Stamps (for Suriname) with RangeMap
Green-tailed jacamar photo gallery VIREO Photo-High Res

The '''green-tailed jacamar''' ('''''Galbula galbula''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Galbulidae]]. It is native to [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], [[French Guiana]], [[Guyana]], [[Suriname]], and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=IOC11.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.1) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=January 2021 |access-date=January 14, 2021 }}</ref><ref name=GTJA-BOW>Tobias, J., T. Züchner, and T.A. de Melo Júnior (2020). Green-tailed Jacamar (''Galbula galbula''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.grtjac1.01 retrieved May 10, 2021</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The green-tailed jacamar is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC11.1/> It and the [[Rufous-tailed jacamar|rufous-tailed]] (''Galbula ruficauda''), [[White-chinned jacamar|white-chinned]] (''G. tombacea''), [[Bluish-fronted jacamar|bluish-fronted]] (''G. cyanescens''), and [[coppery-chested jacamar]]s (''G. pastazae'') are considered to form a [[superspecies]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, J. F. Pacheco, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 19 January 2021. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved January 19, 2021</ref>

[[File:Galbula galbula - Keulemans.jpg|thumb|200px|left|''Galbula galbula'', female (below) and male (above), illustration by [[John Gerrard Keulemans|Keulemans]]]]

==Description==

The green-tailed jacamar is {{convert|18|to|22|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|18|to|29|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The male's head, upperparts, and a band across the chest are metallic coppery green, somewhat bluer on the face. The tail is bluish green. The throat is white and the belly and vent area reddish chestnut. The female is similar but the throat is buff and the underparts are duller and paler.<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The green-tailed jacamar is found from eastern Colombia's [[Vichada Department|Vichada]] and [[Meta Department]]s east through southern and eastern Venezuela south of the [[Orinoco River]] to [[the Guianas]] and south in Brazil to the lower [[Madeira River|Madeira]] and [[Tapajós River]]s. It inhabits the borders, but not the interior, of several humid forest types including ''[[Forest#Tropical moist|terra firme]]'', ''[[várzea forest|várzea]]'', [[gallery forest|gallery]], and [[mangrove]]. It prefers open woodland, [[savanna]], and shrublands, especially along watercourses. In elevation it ranges from sea level to {{convert|700|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===

The green-tailed jacamar's diet is a large variety of insects though [[Hymenoptera]] predominate. It perches on exposed branches, often in pairs, and sallies from there to catch its flying prey.<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The green-tailed jacamar excavates burrows in earth banks or arboreal termite nests. It has been documented breeding February to March in Venezuela, in May, June, and August in Suriname, and in April and September in Brazil.<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>

===Vocalization===

The green-tailed jacamar's song is an "accelerating 'peeo peeo peea pee-pee-pee-pee-pe-pe-pe-e-e-e-e'e'e'e' that ends in a trill [https://www.xeno-canto.org/610367]. Its call is a repeated "peep" or "peer", sometimes in a series [https://www.xeno-canto.org/24205].<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the green-tailed jacamar as being of Least Concern.<ref name=IUCN/> It appears to be common in most of its range and occurs in several protected areas.<ref name=GTJA-BOW/>
